did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning methodologies, specifically an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0, combined with a Levy Stable probability density function for skin cancer classification. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images to classify pigmented skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel ensemble technique based on the Levy Stable probability density function and deep learning (MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0) for skin cancer classification. It details the methodology, dataset (HAM10000), implementation using OpenVINO, and reports new experimental results including accuracy (83%) and inference speeds (110 FPS), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Levy Stable Based Ensemble and It’s Real-Time Implementation on OpenVINO Toolkit
journal: 2023 Twelfth International Conference on Image Processing Theory, Tools and Applications (IPTA)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel skin cancer classification method utilizing an ensemble of MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0 deep learning models optimized by a Levy Stable probability density function. The approach was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains dermoscopic images of seven types of pigmented skin lesions. To facilitate real-time deployment, the model was implemented using the Intel OpenVINO toolkit for hardware inferencing. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 83% and an F1-score of 83%. Furthermore, the optimized model achieved high-performance inference rates of 110 FPS and 91 FPS on an Intel i7 CPU using float16 and float32 precision, respectively. The authors conclude that the method effectively enhances classification accuracy while enabling efficient real-time detection on edge devices.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesion; 66.95% of HAM10000 dataset; non-cancerous.
Malignant lesion; 11.11% of HAM10000 dataset; malignant.
Malignant lesion; 5.13% of HAM10000 dataset; malignant.
Malignant lesion; 3.26% of HAM10000 dataset; malignant.
Benign lesion; 1.14% of HAM10000 dataset; non-cancerous.
Benign lesion; 10.97% of HAM10000 dataset; non-cancerous.
Benign lesion; 1.42% of HAM10000 dataset; non-cancerous.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of pigmented skin lesions (Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, Dermatofibroma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer using an ensemble of MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0 models optimized by a Levy Stable probability density function. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with a reported accuracy of 83% and F1-score of 83%.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, made publicly available in 2018. It includes metadata on patient sex, age, and lesion location. The dataset consists of 400x600 pixel images.

use.training: The dataset was resampled using oversampling to reduce class imbalance, then split into three parts for training, validation, and testing.
use.validation: One part of the split data was used for validation.
use.testing: One part of the split data was used for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and inference speed (FPS).

HAM10000.experiment: Skin Cancer Classification using Levy Stable Based Ensemble
HAM10000.notes: The ensemble model outperformed basic models in accuracy, precision, and F1-score. It showed better prediction for MEL and Vasc classes compared to basic models. The highest FPS for the ensemble model was 110.3 FPS on Intel i7 CPU with FP16 compression. MobileNet-V2 on CPU had the highest FPS of 432.6 among all models.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0 optimized by Levy Stable probability density function
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models from popular deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow, Caffe, and ONNX can be used in OpenVINO (implied standard pretraining for MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0, but specific pretraining dataset not explicitly stated in context)

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Levy Stable probability density function (PDF) with robust ranking mechanism

training_details.data_preprocessing: Oversampling to reduce class imbalance; split into training, validation, and testing
training_details.ensemble_training: Base models utilized without additional training for the Levy Stable function

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Levy Stable Based Ensemble (Ensemble of MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0)
preprocessing_techniques: Oversampling (data augmentation) to reduce class imbalance
Data splitting into training, validation, and testing sets
FP16 and FP32 compression using Model Optimizer for hardware inferencing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to reduce class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset before splitting into training, validation, and testing sets. No specific geometric or color transform parameters were reported.

conclusions: The proposed Levy Stable based ensemble method, combining MobileNet-V2 and EfficientNet-B0, outperforms state-of-the-art methods and basic models in skin cancer classification accuracy.
The ensemble model achieved an accuracy, precision, and F1-score of 83%, which is higher than MobileNet-V2 (82%) and EfficientNet-B0 (77%).
The Levy Stable Ensemble model demonstrated superior prediction capabilities for Melanoma (MEL) and Vascular (Vasc) classes compared to the basic models.
The model was successfully optimized for real-time hardware inferencing using the Intel OpenVINO toolkit.
The optimized ensemble model achieved high-performance inference rates of 110.31 FPS on an Intel i7 CPU with FP16 precision and 91.12 FPS with FP32 precision.
The use of the Model Optimizer to convert models to FP16 compression reduced the model file size by half without a significant drop in precision.

The implementation enables efficient real-time detection on edge devices and Intel hardware platforms (CPU and GPU), addressing challenges in deploying deep learning models across different hardware architectures.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., NV at 66.95% vs. DF at 1.14%) and multi-source bias; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.
The reported accuracy of 83% is modest for a critical medical task like skin cancer classification, particularly given the high stakes of false negatives in melanoma detection.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate that the performance gains of the ensemble model over baseline models are statistically significant.

The model was trained and evaluated on 32x32 pixel images, which is a very low resolution for dermoscopic analysis and may discard fine-grained diagnostic features necessary for accurate classification.

The hardware inference tests were conducted on specific Intel hardware (i7 CPU, iRIS Xe GPU) and Google Colab T4 GPU; results may not generalize to other hardware architectures or edge devices without further testing.
The paper acknowledges that accuracy can be further improved, indicating the current model may not be sufficient for standalone clinical deployment without human-in-the-loop verification.
No details are provided regarding data leakage prevention mechanisms beyond standard train/validation/test splits, raising potential concerns given the use of oversampling augmentation.
